d: Japanese‑to‑English Transformer‑Align MT model from the Opus corpus, using normalization and SentencePiece preprocessing, achieves 41.7 BLEU and 0.589 chr‑F on the Tatoeba test set.

A BERT-based model (derived from bert‑base‑japanese‑char‑extended) pre‑trained on Japanese Wikipedia for UPOS‑feats tagging and dependency parsing, usable via Torch‑Transformers and the esupar tokenizer.
Fine‑tuned, GGUF‑quantized LFM2‑350M checkpoint for near real‑time bi‑directional Japanese‑English translation of short‑to‑medium text, usable via llama.cpp.

LFM2.5‑1.2B‑JP is a Japanese‑optimized chat model that outperforms LFM2 on Japanese knowledge and instruction‑following, supports fine‑tuning with LoRA, inference via Transformers, vLLM, and llama.cpp, and achieves 50.7 JMMLU, 58.1 M‑IFEval, and 56.0 GSM8K scores.

LFM2.5‑1.2B‑JP is a 1.2 B‑parameter Japanese text generation model built on the LFM2.5 hybrid architecture, optimized for generation and completion tasks, hosted on Hugging Face and runnable via llama.cpp.
Fine‑tuned BERT for Japanese Twitter sentiment analysis on the JTS1k dataset, classifying tweets into negative (0), neutral (1), and positive (2) labels, ready for use with the transformers pipeline.

Anime‑XCodec2‑44.1kHz‑v2 upsamples 16 kHz Japanese speech to 44.1 kHz high‑fidelity audio with a decoder‑only RMS‑loss fine‑tune, keeping the encoder/codebook frozen and preserving identical speech tokens.

TinySwallow‑1.5B is a compact Japanese instruction‑following language model by Sakana AI and the Swallow Team that uses TAID distillation from Qwen2.5‑32B‑Instruct, is further pre‑trained on Japanese text, and is released under Apache 2.0 for research use only.
TinySwallow‑1.5B‑Instruct is a 1.5 B Japanese instruction‑tuned autoregressive language model distilled with TAID from Qwen2.5‑32B‑Instruct, intended for research use only.
Provides GGUF‑formatted, hardware‑quantised model files for Stability AI’s 70‑billion‑parameter Japanese StableLM Instruct Beta, ready for use with LLaMA‑cpp‑based tools.
Repository provides GGUF‑formatted, quantised model files for Stability AI’s Japanese StableLM Instruct Gamma 7B, created with Massed Compute hardware and part of TheBloke’s a16z‑funded LLM work.
s: Vocabulary, Dictionary, and Language Input Method

d: A 2.7‑B‑parameter Japanese GPT‑NeoX model trained on Japanese CC‑100 and OSCAR by ABEJA Inc, usable via Hugging Face Transformers pipelines or PyTorch and released under the MIT license.

d: A large Japanese GPT‑2 model trained by ABEJA, Inc. on Japanese CC‑100, Wikipedia, and OSCAR, tokenized with sentencepiece, usable via Hugging Face transformers in PyTorch or TensorFlow under the MIT license.
s: Text Generation, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: Fine‑tuned the Japanese Stable LM Base Gamma 7B with Shisa 7B data, achieving strong results on the JA MT‑Bench.

A Japanese TTS model named **tsukuyomi‑wavlm**—fine‑tuned for 300 epochs on 100 utterances from the tsukuyomi corpus, using the WavLM discriminator and A1/A2/A3 prosody features on a VITS architecture, exported as a 61 MB ONNX file that generates 22.05 kHz syntheses.

JaColBERTv2 is a Japanese‑only ColBERT‑based retrieval model trained with knowledge distillation on MMarco (31 negatives per positive, 250k steps, batch 32) that currently outperforms multilingual‑e5‑large, BGE‑M3, and JaColBERT, with a full evaluation pending.
Fine‑tuned Japanese BERT (cl‑tohoku/bert‑base‑japanese‑v2) on Amazon product reviews for sentiment classification, achieving ~81 % accuracy and 0.73 F1 after 6 epochs with a 2 × 10⁻⁵ learning rate.
s: Representation Learning, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing, Sentiment Analysis

s: Representation Learning, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: The repository delivers new Japanese text‑embedding v3 models (30M‑310M params, 8 k‑token max, JMTEB 74‑77) that can be loaded with sentence_transformers (using query/passage prefixes) and includes benchmark comparisons with supervised/unsupervised SimCSE, LaBSE, E5, and other models.

A collection of release‑ready Ruri v3 Japanese text embedding models (30m–310m), complete with SentenceTransformer usage tips, query/passage prefixes, and JMTEB benchmark results showing how they compare to other Japanese and multilingual embeddings.
s: Representation Learning, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: A Japanese cross‑encoder reranker built with Sentence Transformers that demonstrates inference usage and benchmark results for various sized Ruri‑Reranker models.

Includes Ruri v3 Japanese text embeddings (30 M–310 M parameters, 8192‑token limit, JMTEB 74.5–77.2), instructions for Sentence Transformers using “クエリ:” or “文章:” prefixes, and benchmark results for several Japanese models such as Sup/Unsup SimCSE, GLuCoSE, and LaBSE.

Ruri v3 is a state‑of‑the‑art Japanese text‑embedding model built on ModernBERT‑Ja, supporting up to 8192‑token sequences, a 100K‑token vocabulary, FlashAttention, and released in sizes from 30 M to 310 M parameters for use with sentence‑transformers.

Ruri v3 is a state‑of‑the‑art Japanese text embedding model built on ModernBERT‑Ja, supporting up to 8,192 tokens, a 100 k‑token vocabulary, FlashAttention acceleration, and multiple sizes from 37 M to 315 M parameters.

Ruri v3 is a state‑of‑the‑art Japanese text‑embedding model built on ModernBERT‑Ja, supporting up to 8,192‑token inputs, a 100K‑token vocabulary, FlashAttention‑accelerated inference, and multiple size variants for fast sentence‑transformer usage.

Ruri v3 delivers high‑performance Japanese text embeddings up to 8192 tokens, a 100k‑token vocabulary, FlashAttention support, and multiple model sizes (30 m–310 m) for efficient inference and fine‑tuning via sentence‑transformers.

Ruri‑v3 Reranker is a robust Japanese text reranker built on ModernBERT‑Ja, supporting up to 8,192‑token sequences, a 100k‑token vocabulary, FlashAttention and a SentencePiece tokenizer, and it can be used via sentence‑transformers.

A Japanese BERT‑base model fine‑tuned with supervised SimCSE on JSNLI, exposed via Sentence‑Transformers or HuggingFace with CLS pooling, trained on 1 M examples at 512‑batch size, 5 × 10⁻⁵ learning rate, 5 × 10⁻⁵ temperature, 64‑token limit, and BFloat16 precision.

Sup‑simcse‑ja‑large provides a supervised SimCSE fine‑tuned Japanese BERT‑large (cl‑tohoku/bert‑large‑japanese‑v2) model with CLS‑plus‑MLP pooling, trained on ~1 M JSNLI sentences (lr 5e‑5, batch 512, temp 0.05, max 64) and ready for use with Sentence‑Transformers or Hugging Face Transformers.

A Japanese continually‑pre‑trained Mistral‑Nemo model (Mistral‑Nemo‑Japanese‑Instruct‑2408) built on mistralai/Mistral‑Nemo‑Instruct‑2407, usable via transformers with device mapping and ChatML prompts, released under Apache‑2.0 by Ryosuke Ishigami.

OpenCALM is a suite of decoder‑only Japanese transformer language models (160 M–6.8 B params) released by CyberAgent, Inc. under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0, trained on Japanese Wikipedia and Common Crawl, and usable via Hugging Face’s torch‑transformers.
s: Syntactic Text Processing, Text Segmentation, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: OpenCALM is a Japanese decoder‑only transformer language‑model suite from CyberAgent, Inc. featuring versions ranging from 160 M to 6.8 B parameters pre‑trained on Wikipedia and Common Crawl, available via the Transformers library under a CC BY‑SA 4.0 license.
OpenCALM is a family of Japanese decoder‑only Transformer language models (160 M–6.8 B parameters) from CyberAgent, trained on Japanese Wikipedia and Common Crawl and released under CC BY‑SA 4.0.
s: Syntactic Text Processing, Text Segmentation, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: A gguf‑quantized Qwen2.5‑3B‑Instruct model employing a Japanese‑rich iMatrix that supports accurate summarization of texts longer than 32 K tokens, retains extensive Japanese capability, and allows context windows up to 128 K through configurable parameters (e.g., for Ollama).

Qwen3.5-4B‑UD‑japanese‑imatrix by dahara1 is a top‑tier, Japanese‑focused GGUF model using Unsloth Dynamic Quantization 2.0 with extensive Japanese calibration and community‑fixed bugs, runnable on llama.cpp with at least 8 GB RAM and 3 GB disk even without GPU.

A Qwen 3.5‑9B GGUF model fine‑tuned for Japanese, employing Unsloth Dynamic Quantization 2.0, extensive bug fixes, large‑scale Japanese calibration, and runnable on CPU with 16 GB RAM and 6 GB disk via llama.cpp.

A GGUF‑converted Gemma‑4‑E2B‑it model fine‑tuned for Japanese proficiency, built with Unsloth® Dynamic Quantization 2.0, community bug fixes, Japanese calibration data, and runnable on a CPU (≥8 GB RAM, ≥4 GB disk) via llama.cpp.

A highly optimized GGUF version of google/gemma‑4‑E4B‑it, tuned for Japanese language proficiency using Unsloth Dynamic Quantization 2.0 and extensive bug fixes, that runs on llama.cpp with at least 16 GB RAM and 6 GB disk (GPU optional).

A GGUF‑quantized shisa‑v2.1‑qwen3‑8b model built with Unsloth Dynamic 2.0, community‑patched Qwen3 settings to reduce malfunctions, a larger imatrix for stronger Japanese performance, and a 40K maximum context length.

A 39,600‑sample Japanese creative writing dataset built with deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3-0324, each entry containing two OpenAI‑style message turns (instruction_1/output_1 and instruction_2/output_2), some NSFW content, and released under MIT license.
A 97,269‑record Japanese dialogue dataset generated by applying Magpie’s method to weblab‑GENIAC/Tanuki‑8B‑dpo‑v1.0, without post‑filtering, so it may contain low‑quality entries.

A 190,854‑item Japanese synthetic preference dataset built with five open models and Qwen/Qwen2.5‑72B‑Instruct‑GPTQ‑Int8, using pairwise judging (with reversed order) to select only consistent pairs, released under Apache 2.0 but subject to Qwen license constraints for model training.
Japanese QA dataset generated by GPT‑4 Self‑Instruct with basic filtering and semantic similarity checks, intended for fine‑tuning open‑source models and continuously updated.

JMedBench is a Japanese biomedical LLM benchmark comprising 20 datasets across five tasks (MCQA, NER, STS, etc.) sourced from MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU, and others, each with its own license, and includes a note that translations may contain biases requiring human review.
Large compressed JSON‑Lines dataset of anonymous 2ch.sc/2ch.net threads, including thread IDs, titles, board and region details, reply counts, and full post metadata (author, mail, date, content).

A 3‑turn Japanese knowledge QA dataset (~3,000 JSONL entries) created with the SDG‑LOOM pipeline, DeepSeek V3.2 for question refinement and follow‑ups, Kimi K2.5 for answers, and dynamic reasoning difficulty labels, licensed under ODC‑BY.

A 7,000‑item Japanese single‑turn knowledge QA dataset generated from Aratako/Synthetic‑JP‑Conversations‑Magpie‑Nemotron‑4‑10k using DeepSeek V3.2 for question refinement and Kimi K2.5 with dynamic reasoning (low/medium/high) to produce answers, all stored in JSONL under an ODC‑BY license.

108 k Japanese instruction‑response pairs created with the BARE method, combining Sarashina2‑70B for queries, Qwen3‑235B‑A22B for answers, and filtered by Multilingual‑E5‑Large and Phi‑4, delivered in JSON Lines format.

A non‑official dataset of Hololive VTuber Sakura Miko’s voice for speech‑recognition research, organized by source platform and split into train/test folders, licensed under Hololive’s fan‑creation guidelines with all rights held by Cover Corporation.

Japanese CallHome corpus: 120 speakers recorded 200 phone‑call conversations (30 min max) in the U.S., transcribed into 80 training, 20 dev‑test, and 100 eval‑test sets, with recordings available via a toll‑free LDC robot operator.

Hachi‑Alpaca is a Japanese synthetic dataset created from Stanford Alpaca with ‑mistralai/Mixtral‑8x22B‑Instruct‑v0.1, cleaned by that model, served on Deepinfra, and released under the Apache‑2.0 license for Alpaca‑jp research.

alpaca_jp_python is a Japanese synthetic Alpaca dataset created with mistralai/Mixtral‑8x22B‑Instruct‑v0.1, cleaned by the same model, hosted on Deepinfra, and released under the HachiML Japanese Apache 2.0 license.

A 10,000‑sample paired dataset of ~30 hours of Japanese audio with aligned Simplified Chinese text in Parquet format (CC BY 4.0), designed for speech‑to‑text translation and multilingual ASR+MT research.

JMMMU is a Japanese multimodal benchmark expanded over tenfold to 1,320 culturally diverse questions (720 culture‑agnostic, 600 culture‑specific) translated by native subject experts, now featuring a public leaderboard.

JMMMU‑Pro is a Japanese multimodal benchmark built via Vibe Construction—combining generative modeling and human verification—to generate low‑cost, high‑quality image‑QA data that exposes open‑source LMMs’ shortcomings and steers future VQA research.

Aggregates over 150 GB of NicoNico Live’s comment logs from 2009‑2024—including pre‑transition, post‑transition, and real‑time NX‑Jikkyo captures—providing an API for easy retrieval of historical TV‑broadcast discussions.
s: Vocabulary, Dictionary, and Language Input Method

d: AnimuSubtitle‑JP is a Japanese subtitle dataset in Advanced SubStation Alpha format, usable via the Python ‘ass’ library or editors like Aegisub, and licensed under ODC‑By.

A Japanese web‑scale dataset of 3.88 M images with LLM‑generated captions and open‑ended QA pairs, filtered for deduplication and enriched by detection tags, available in multiple variants (simple, refined, detection, all) and hosted on Hugging Face.

Japanese Medical VQA 12M is a commercial‑usable multimodal dataset of over 12 million Japanese medical images and captions, derived from Open‑PMC‑18M, included in Parquet/Webdataset format, and containing original and Japanese‑translated images, enriched captions, and VQA‐style Q&A pairs generated u

A large‑scale, JSONL dataset of ~190 M Japanese “left context–input–output” triples for kana‑to‑kanji conversion, enabling training of the zenz‑v2.5 series (medium, small, xsmall) and providing the AJIMEE‑Bench evaluation benchmark under CC BY‑SA 4.0 or Common Crawl licenses.

A partial dataset of *Project Sekai* vocal tracks for 草薙寧々 (source CV Machico) including a nene_org.txt label file, with plans to expand and standardise the data, and a call to star the repo, share ideas and join a QQ group for the full collection.
s: Vocabulary, Dictionary, and Language Input Method

d: 266‑hour Japanese audio dataset from the Emilia collection, pre‑filtered by Facebook Audio Aesthetics and classified via Scribe v1/ElevenLabs STT, hosted on Hugging Face and open for collaboration on Discord.

A 409‑hour Japanese eroge voice dataset processed with 2‑pass loudnorm (‑23 LUFS, ‑1 dB peak, 11 LRA), transcribed by litagin/anime-whisper, anonymized, stored as WebDataset (FLAC, JSON, TXT), largely featuring female voices with potential AI transcription errors, and MIT‑licensed for academic resea
Japanese‑Eroge‑Voice‑V2 offers 2,657 hours of anonymized 1,033,142 eroge audio–transcription pairs (mostly female, NSFW), MIT‑licensed for academic research.

Sentence‑aligned Japanese web novel chapters and English fan translations (106 K chapters, V2), with updated alignment, added series metadata, retained titles, no quality filtering, licensed Apache 2.0 under fair‑use, and a HuggingFace‑based takedown procedure.
A dataset of 23 million personally gathered Japanese web novels totaling 80.85 billion characters, available only for bona fide machine‑learning use and requiring a detailed access request.

Dataset of Japanese role‑play dialogues filtered to include only multi‑poster records of sufficient length, normalized poster names, and balanced top speakers, intended for machine‑learning applications.

J-HARD‑TTS‑Eval benchmarks the robustness of autoregressive Japanese TTS models on short‑sequence stability, repetition handling, and context completion, with datasets available via Hugging Face (short, repetition, rhyme, continuation).

High‑quality, manually crafted Japanese dialogue dataset focused on tsundere loli themes, featuring short, emotionally rich exchanges in JSONL format for fine‑tuning chat models such as Gemma, Qwen, Llama, Mistral, and OpenHermes under an MIT license.

A dataset card for the SNOW T15/T23 Japanese simplification corpus, containing 50 k manually aligned original, simplified Japanese (≤2 k‑word vocab), and English translation records, plus a 35 k expansion set, for use in Japanese‑English text simplification and translation.

EDINET‑Bench is a Japanese financial benchmark that evaluates LLMs on tasks such as accounting fraud detection, earnings forecasting, and industry prediction using ten years of EDINET‑API disclosed reports, with construction and evaluation code provided and the dataset relicensed to PDL 1.0.
JA‑Multi‑Image‑VQA is a 39‑image, 55‑question dataset with manually crafted Japanese Q&A for multi‑image VQA, accessed via load_dataset, licensed Apache 2.0 for text (images are restricted).
JA‑VG‑VQA‑500 is a 500‑sample subset of the Japanese Visual Genome VQA dataset, licensed CC BY 4.0, used to benchmark EvoVLM‑JP‑v1‑7B.
s: Visual Data in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: The “Regexp.ja” Wiki page in the MeCab‑IPADIC‑Neologd repository explains Japanese regex rules for stripping unnecessary punctuation and normalizing text.
JaQuAD is a 2022 Japanese QA dataset of 39,696 SQuAD‑style extractive pairs from Wikipedia, totaling 73.2 MB, that achieves 78.92 % F1 (63.38 % EM) when fine‑tuned with BERT‑Japanese.
s: Natural Language Interfaces, Question Answering, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Dataset of dialogues and lore from the Fate/Stay Night character “Emilia”, formatted for training and evaluating conversational language models.

KokoroChat is the largest Japanese psychological counseling dialogue dataset—6,589 role‑playing sessions by 480 trained counselors, averaging 91 utterances each—featuring rich, long‑form conversations, detailed 20‑dimensional client feedback, and supports research in empathetic response generation,

A cleaned Japanese‑to‑English translation corpus is provided along with a fine‑tuned Mistral/Stable‑LM model that evaluates each pair, accepting only high‑quality translations and rejecting any incomplete, inaccurate, or poorly written ones.
A gated, non‑commercial derivative dataset of EDINET XBRL financial data that prohibits redistribution, AI training, and commercial use, requiring manual approval for access.

Repository providing the 1.56 B‑token pre‑training dataset (from various licensed public corpora) used to train the AKU‑d_ms‑0.5B‑chat‑v0.1 model in the AKU series, with accompanying processing scripts.
AI‑generated anime illustrations with English prompts and Phi‑3 Vision‑derived captions (English and Japanese) released into the public domain for free use.
s: Captioning, Text Generation, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: A simple dataset of Zundamon character settings—compiled from online sources and admin data—for testing character‑LLMs, provided in zmnjp.jsonl and zmn.jsonl formats under a specified license.
s: Vocabulary, Dictionary, and Language Input Method

d: Provides a Japanese RAG benchmark across five industry domains—finance, telecom, manufacturing, public, retail—by publishing datasets, an automatic evaluation framework, and comparative results for models such as Claude 3.5‑Sonnet, GPT‑4o, and others.

The repository hosts an 81‑year‑old Japanese woman’s voice corpus—raw and noise‑reduced WAV files, phoneme and kana labels—available via a Google Drive link, freely usable for research and commercial speech synthesis with attribution, but disallowing direct file links or pornographic use.

Syosetu711K is a Japanese dataset of ~711,700 novels scraped from 小説家になろう on March 26‑27 2023, providing full text and metadata (title, author, NCode, synopsis, etc.) for unsupervised text generation and classification tasks.
A 2735‑file WAV voice dataset for so‑vits‑svc 4.0, licensed CC‑BY‑NC 4.0 with attribution and noncommercial restrictions, available by email request for research use only.

AutoWikiQA is the largest free Japanese QA dataset, providing over 2.3 million handcrafted‑filtered question–answer pairs automatically generated from Wikipedia with Swallow‑MX and LLMs (without rule‑based templates) to support knowledge‑teaching and retrieval‑augmented generation applications.
COVID‑19 Japanese Twitter dataset supplies Japanese tweet IDs and assessment codes (63–68) indicating COVID‑19 relevance and fact/opinion status, enabling text‑classification research.
A Japanese online‑advertising dataset for five NLP tasks—ad acceptability, consistency, performance estimation, A3 recognition, and similarity—with train/dev/test splits in TSV format.
JP Voice‑Text Dataset for Arknights Waifus: 10,905 Japanese voice clips (26.3 h total) from single‑actor characters, useful for fine‑tuning or evaluating ASR/ASV models.
A Japanese voice‑text dataset of Azur Lane characters featuring 30,160 recordings (75.8 h) from single‑actor voices for ASR/ASV fine‑tuning and evaluation.
Japanese voice‑text dataset for Fate/Grand Order characters, 30,800 clips (≈66.4 h total) featuring single‑actor recordings ideal for ASR/ASV fine‑tuning and evaluation.
JP Voice‑Text Dataset for Girls Front Line Waifus: 12,508 recordings (20.9 h) of single‑actor female characters, ideal for ASR/ASV fine‑tuning and evaluation.

A 100‑sample Japanese instruction‑tuning evaluation dataset of annotated tasks—ranging from summarization correction and math reasoning to translation, creative generation, and user‑intent understanding—designed for manual or automatic 5‑point rating of fine‑tuned models.

A high‑quality synthetic Japanese math problem dataset with verified chain‑of‑thought reasoning, built by translating PRM800K and GSM8K via Qwen2‑7B‑Instruct and filtering for correctness, available through the Hugging Face datasets library.
Japanese Alpaca Data, derived from masa3141’s Japanese Alpaca‑LoRA work, with a reference to the original repository for additional details.
A user‑friendly, deduplicated CSV dataset of public‑domain Japanese texts from Aozora Bunko, processed with globis‑org/aozorabunko‑extractor and cleaned for modern‑Japanese machine‑learning use.
A Nekopara audio dataset providing 44.1 kHz recordings labeled by character name, volume, original file name, transcription, and an is_adult flag that requires careful use for NSFW filtering.
A MangaOCR dataset comprising roughly 209 K narrative text instances from the Manga109 and manga onomatopoeia collections, featuring diverse visual styles and layouts.

A 2010 Japanese Web Corpus, uploaded to HuggingFace and licensed for research per the 2009 copyright reform, includes automatically punctuated text from morphology-based parsing and conversion scripts.
Japanese anime speech dataset, split from the original `joujiboi/japanese-anime-speech-v2` collection.

JFWIR is a 64‑million‑pair Japanese information‑retrieval dataset built from fine‑web educational crawls, offering seven query types per document, hard negatives, and demonstrably better benchmark performance than prior baselines.

A Japanese QA dataset for evaluating Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG), built from JAQKET questions and Wikipedia passages with gold retrieval‑relevance labels, released on HuggingFace and GitHub and scored primarily by nDCG@10.
s: Natural Language Interfaces, Information Retrieval, Question Answering

d: Merged line‑split cc100‑ja data into document units for HuggingFace while retaining the original CC100 license.

FineWeb2 Edu Japanese delivers ~120 million high‑quality educational Japanese texts (≈89.3 billion tokens) from FineWeb2, filtered by a DeepSeek‑API classifier (score ≥ 2.5), tokenized via ModernBERT‑Ja‑130M, and includes a small‑token subset (≤512 tokens).
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: A Japanese hard‑negative mining dataset derived from the MS MARCO translation, filtered and re‑ranked with BAAI/bge‑reranker‑v2‑m3 and scored by japanese‑splade‑base‑v1‑mmarco‑only, shows statistically significant higher positive rates than mMARCO(ja) at 0.7/0.8 thresholds (≈ 1.3–1.7 % improvement).

Converted Japanese datasets into SentenceTransformers‑friendly columns, filtering examples by Rerank scores (≥0.7 positive, ≤0.3 negative) from multiple HuggingFace sources to support contrastive learning while respecting the original licenses.

Japanese Wikipedia sentences are transformed into various embeddings and a FAISS index, offering a Hugging Face Space demo, conversion scripts, and evaluations of search, Q&A, and OpenAI text‑embedding‑3‑small for RAG; embeddings are OpenAI‑licensed, others CC‑BY‑SA‑4.0.
A catalog of Japanese and multilingual QA, NLI and paraphrase datasets, detailing each dataset’s retrieval or QA tasks and its license (Apache 2.0, CC‑BY‑SA/CC‑BY, MIT, etc.).

A NLP‑ready dataset of Wikipedia articles extracted from a January 2024 HTML dump that preserves paragraph structure, metadata (such as disambiguation, sexual, violent flags, templates, timestamps) and accompanying extraction scripts hosted on GitHub.
A Japanese‑Wikipedia–based bullet‑point dataset generated with the Apache 2.0‑licensed rinna/deepseek‑r1‑distill‑qwen2.5‑bakeneko‑32b model, distributed under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0.
A Japanese‑to‑English parallel corpus translating the kaken subset of llm‑jp‑corpus‑v3 with Qwen/Qwen2.5‑32B‑Instruct, featuring custom translation columns and licensed under CC‑BY‑4.0.
Converted the kaken subset of llm-jp-corpus-v3 to Hugging Face format and appended retrieved article titles from each URL where possible, licensed CC‑BY 4.0.
Dataset of LLM‑generated Japanese queries and answers created from paraphrased Wikipedia text, released under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0.
Japanese Wikipedia Q&A dataset with LLM‑generated questions and answers from DeepSeek‑R1‑Distill‑Qwen‑32B‑Japanese, released under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0.

Provides a Japanese search/QA dataset with per‑query scores computed by five multilingual/Japanese rerankers (e.g., BAAI/bge‑reranker‑v2‑m3, Alibaba‑NLP/gte‑multilingual‑reranker‑base), including average scores for roughly 200 positive and negative example documents per query.
Loads a 312,396‑row filtered subset of the Oscar 2023 dataset from Hugging Face (if001/oscar_2023_filtered) and provides sample code in the if001/HojiChar_OSCAR_sample GitHub repository.

Japanese instruction‑chat dataset for fine‑tuning LLMs (e.g., with LoRA), 9 M+ samples, recently updated to drop licensed Alpaca data, clean Wikipedia and ALT outputs, and released under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0.

A Japanese chat dataset for instruction‑response fine‑tuning (e.g., via LoRA), derived from llm‑japanese‑dataset by excluding Japanese‑English translation examples, offering 1.8–2.5 million entries across versioned releases and licensed under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0 with citation DOI 10.1109/BigData59044.2023.
Dataset card for the Japanese MC4 dataset (mc4-ja).
Dataset card detailing the Japanese‑language variant “mc4‑ja‑filter‑ja‑normal”, with additional information pending.
Dataset card for “oscar2301‑ja‑filter‑ja‑normal,” a Japanese filtered and normal subset of the Oscar corpus.
Dataset card for the “wikipedia-ja-20230720” Japanese Wikipedia snapshot.

A Japanese a cappella vocal ensemble corpus featuring six‑voice parts (lead, soprano, alto, tenor, bass, and percussion) with scores, isolated recordings, and genre‑divided subsets spanning jazz, punk rock, bossa nova, popular music, reggae, enka, neutral, ballad, EDM, and soul/funk.

A Japanese Wikipedia dataset (209,496 items) was processed with Qwen/Qwen2‑72B‑Instruct using chain‑of‑thought generation under a CC‑BY‑SA‑3.0/GNU Free Documentation license, supported by TPU Research Cloud.

Restructured reupload of the Galgame VisualNovel dataset (OOPPEENN/56697375616C4E6F76656C5F44617461736574) for efficient Hugging Face datasets loading, preserving all original audio/text and providing an extraction script with multiple game‑subset options.

Japanese Anime Speech Dataset offers 73,004 audio‑text pairs (110 hours total, evolving from V1 to V5) to enhance ASR models such as OpenAI’s Whisper, available under an open license for all uses with credit appreciated.
s: Speech Recognition, Text Generation, Speech & Audio in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Japanese Anime Speech Dataset V2 delivers 292,637 cleaned audio‑text pairs—about 397.5 h of SFW and 52.4 h of NSFW content—in 128‑kbps MP3 files split by safety, designed specifically for training automatic speech‑recognition models.
s: Speech Recognition, Text Generation, Speech & Audio in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Hugging Face mirror of the ABEJA‑CC‑JA open‑data set from AWS (https://registry.opendata.aws/abeja-cc-ja/) with details in Abeja’s technical blog.
A Japanese news corpus from July–October 2024 extracted from Common Crawl’s news subset using Uzushio with pipeline_03a.conf filtering settings.

A cleaned Japanese web corpus (e.g., mc4‑ja) clustered into roughly 10 000 groups by unsupervised learning, usable for lawful analysis, with only some files in Parquet format listed in the “out” folder and downloadable via Git LFS.

A 20,800‑file WAV dataset of 195 Japanese AItuber personas—including reference, original, descriptive, and emotional utterances—is supplied with detailed persona and voice metadata, ready for retrieval via data‑science APIs.

JHumanEval is a hand‑translated Japanese version of the HumanEval benchmark, providing 164 Python programming problems with parallel English and Japanese comments to evaluate Japanese‑LLM code generation while preserving the original English errors.
s: Multilinguality, Text Generation, Machine Translation, Language Models, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: JAQKET is a Japanese open‑domain QA dataset derived from Wikipedia, offering version 1.0 with multiple‑choice quiz questions (13,061 training, 271 validation examples) and version 2.0 with only question prompts requiring extracted answers (2,154 training, 1,164 validation), designed to facilitate re
s: Natural Language Interfaces, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: A 1.8‑million‑entry Japanese translation of the OpenMathInstruct‑1 math instruction tuning dataset, generated from GSM8K and MATH benchmark questions paired with Mixtral‑8x7B solutions, filtered for correctness, and released under a commercial NVIDIA license that must be inherited on redistribution.

A 5.2 k‑item instruction dataset focused on code, now adding 180 JaxTon and professional‑Java entries, comprising 1,050 code‑generation, 150 code‑behavior‑check and 4,000 code‑fix records, assembled from commercially licensed learning content that has been translated into Japanese and fully authoriz
An automatically translated Japanese version of the databricks‑dolly‑15k dataset, licensed CC‑BY‑SA‑3.0 and last updated on 2023‑05‑11.
s: Multilinguality, Text Generation, Machine Translation, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: This repository hosts a Japanese translation of the OpenAssistant/oasst1 dataset, including auto‑translated entries with error flags, roughly 2,000 manual corrections, a chat‑formatted subset, and scripts to convert the data into instruction‑output pairs for fine‑tuning.

Japanese‑only MGE‑universe stories pre‑approved and chronologically sorted, each with metadata (chapter, title, author, summary, tags, views, votes, update date, byte/token lengths) and up to 500 k raw text characters ready for further processing.

Galgame_Speech_ASR_16kHz is a 16 kHz ASR dataset with 3.75 million pairs (≈5,354 h), derived from Galgame_Dataset, released under GPL v3.0 with commercial use prohibited and requiring any trained models to be open‑source (citation optional).

Galgame_Speech_SER_16kHz is a 3.7 million‑file (5,353‑hour, 104 GB) emotional speech dataset derived from Galgame_Speech_ASR_16kHz, annotated by a local LLM, released under GNU GPL v3.0 with commercial use prohibited, and requiring models trained on it to be open‑source without mandatory citation.

A mirror of the Reazon Speech v2 Japanese dataset hosted on Hugging Face, distributed under CDLA‑Sharing‑1.0 with use restricted to Japanese Copyright Act Article 30‑4, containing 4,096 16 kHz FLAC audio files and matching transcriptions in TSV/CSV format.

A mirrored release of the Reazon Speech v2 dataset, containing audio files that have been denoised and background‑music‑removed via UVR (3674 of 4096 files processed on eight A800 GPUs over roughly ten days).

Dataset card for the JGLUE dataset used in the book “Large Language Model Introduction,” sourced from the original repo, with code licensed CC BY‑SA 4.0, data under the distributor’s license, citing Kurihara & Kawahara (in Japanese), and built on Shunsuke Kitada’s repository.
A cleaned subset of the livedoor news corpus (licensed CC BY‑ND 2.1 JP) used in the book “Introduction to Large Language Models,” described by the llm‑book/ner‑wikinews‑dataset card.
Dataset card for the ja‑vicuna‑qa‑benchmark used in the book “Introduction to Large‑Scale LLM II” and created by llm‑jp‑eval for cross‑dataset Japanese LLM evaluation (Apache 2.0).
s: Natural Language Interfaces, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Japanese NER dataset “Wikipedia-based Named Entity Extraction” (v2.0) used in the book *Large Language Models: An Introduction*, created by Stockmark and released under CC‑BY‑SA 3.0.

A dataset card for llm‑book/wrime‑sentiment offering a binary Japanese sentiment analysis set derived from WRIME, labeled as positive or negative based on Avg. Readers_Sentiment (with an option to include neutral cases), and intended as sample data for the book “Introduction to Large Language Models

AnswerCarefully Dataset supplies Japanese and multilingual data for commercial or non‑commercial LLM safety enhancement, prohibits any other use—including safety circumvention—allows derivative works with attribution, and carries a creator disclaimer of non‑liability for harms or service changes.

JAMMEval is a distilled benchmark of seven Japanese VQA datasets, refined with two rounds of human annotation to remove ambiguities and non‑visual questions, delivering reliable evaluation of vision‑language models on multimodal Japanese tasks.

Jagle is a ~9.2 million‑instance Japanese multimodal post‑training dataset built from image–text pairs and PDF corpora, used to train LLM‑jp‑4‑VL 9B beta and shown to boost performance on Japanese vision‑language tasks.

WAON is a large, high‑quality Japanese image‑text pair dataset for vision‑language models, built with strict filtering (image size, SigLIP scores) and deduplication (URL, caption, perceptual hash), and released under an Apache 2.0 license for “information analysis” use.

The Databricks‑dolly‑15k‑ja dataset is a DeepL‑translated Japanese version of databricks‑dolly‑15k for instruction tuning, created by Japan’s LLM‑jp project and authored by Hirokazu Kiyomaru, Hiroshi Matsuda, Jun Suzuki, Namgi Han, Saku Sugawara, Shota Sasaki, Shuhei Kurita, Taishi Nakamura, Takashi

Instruction‑tuned Japanese dialogue dataset from LLM‑jp, curated from a subset of Japanese Wikipedia and filtered with Qwen/Qwen2.5‑32B‑Instruct, featuring two‑turn and four‑turn formats for use in model training.
llm‑jp‑instructions is a manually curated Japanese instruction dataset (v1.0) offering train, dev, and test splits accessible through load_dataset.
A dataset of 200 Japan‑specific multiple‑choice questions, each with four options (169 single‑correct, 31 multi‑correct) and annotated rationales for which answers differ from other countries.

mbpp‑ja LLM‑jp is a Japanese collaboration that adapts the MBPP dataset with DeepL translation, provides HuggingFace and GitHub links to the source data, lists authors Namgi Han, Masatoshi Otake, Shintaro Ozaki, Yusuke Miyao, and invites questions at llm‑jp(at)nii.ac.jp.

oasst1‑21k‑ja is a Japanese instruction‑tuning dataset derived by DeepL from the English OASST1 subset, created through the LLM‑jp collaborative project in Japan; contact llm‑jp@nii.ac.jp and authors include Kiyomaru, Matsuda, Suzuki, Han, Sugawara, Sasaki, Kurita, Nakamura, Kodama, and Okamoto.
LLM‑jp provides a Japanese instruction‑tuning dataset, DeepL‑translated from an English subset of oasst2 (derived from kunishou/oasst2‑135k‑ja) and compiled by Kiyomaru and Kodama.

A collection of Japanese and English web corpora—including a 9 B‑token Japanese set (JA‑WEB‑9B), 63 B‑token English and Japanese sets (EN‑WEB‑63B, JA‑WEB‑63B) and synthetic versions such as paraphrased JA‑PARAPHRASE‑63B, instruction‑style JA‑INSTRUCT‑63B, and translated JA‑TRANSLATE‑63B—used for stu

Ranking LLMs on how accurately they translate Japanese visual novels into English, the VNTL leaderboard uses cosine‑similarity against 256 reference translations (with chrF reported but not ranked) and reports preliminary results that outperform tools such as Sugoi Translator, Google Translate, Papa

Japanese JaQuAD, a subset of QG‑Bench, provides sentence‑ and paragraph‑level data with highlighted answer tokens for training Japanese question‑generation models, evaluated by BLEU4, METEOR, ROUGE‑L, BERTScore, and MoverScore.
Japanese Common Voice 17.0 subset filtered to 649 client IDs with 30–300 samples each, split 8:2 into train/validation, batched in 1,000‑sample Parquet files, totaling 45,668 samples (CC0 licensed).

Manually extracted QC‑checked FAQ dataset from Japanese government agency websites, licensed CC‑BY‑4.0, presented in QA format with source URLs for LLM instruction‑tuning and RAG testing, yet subject to PDF‑to‑text conversion quirks and strong governmental perspectives.
Japanese XL‑Sum subset filtered via PaLM‑2 15‑gram overlap, containing 4,215 training, 758 validation, and 766 test examples.

Japanese RAG Generator Benchmark (J‑RAGBench) supplies a multi‑category QA dataset—covering Integration, Reasoning, Logical, Table, and Abstention—designed to evaluate Japanese RAG generators, built with human effort and GPT‑4.1, and released under CC BY‑SA 4.0.

JMMLU is a Japanese Massive Multitask Language Understanding Benchmark featuring 7,536 teacher‑crafted questions across 56 subjects, including professional medicine, psychology, accounting, philosophy, and diverse high‑school disciplines.
s: Multilinguality, Text Generation, Machine Translation, Language Models

d: Japanese instruction dataset with 632,636 multi‑turn samples (~6.35 B tokens) and GPT‑OSS‑120b reasoning traces for open‑ended, math, writing, and MCQA tasks across 135 finance topics plus 20 general ones, designed to fine‑tune LLM reasoning in finance.

RealPersonaChat is a 14,000‑utterance Japanese conversation corpus that pairs dialogue records with speaker persona, BigFive personality scores, demographic data, and evaluative ratings (1–5), accessible through Hugging Face’s `load_dataset` but requiring strict privacy and non‑impersonation complia

Nemotron‑Personas‑Japan is an open‑source, CC BY 4.0 dataset of high‑quality, synthetically generated Japanese personas—incorporating name, gender, age, background, marital status, education, occupation and location—grounded in real‑world demographic, geographic and personality distributions, engine

BBH‑ja provides a Japanese translation of the BIG‑Bench Hard dataset, offering evaluation problems in JSON‑L (input, correct target) and Chain‑of‑Thought prompts in YAML (input, target), translated using the PLaMo model.
cc100-ja is a collection of the Japanese portion of the cc100 dataset, provided as sharded Parquet files.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Range3/wiki40b‑ja hosts a Japanese subset of the wiki40b dataset as three Parquet files produced by a Python data‑processing pipeline.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Range3’s wikipedia‑ja‑20230101 repository offers Parquet files containing only Japanese Wikipedia text, extracted from the full Wikipedia dataset and generated with Python code.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: ReazonSpeech is a free, FLAC‑encoded Japanese speech corpus with transcriptions, offered in five sizes from 8.5 h to 35,000 h, downloadable via Hugging Face under the CDLA‑Sharing‑1.0 license and limited to use under Japan Copyright Act Article 30‑4.
s: Speech & Audio in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Four Japanese‑centric image‑classification tasks—jafood101 (101 dishes), jaflower30 (30 flowers), jafacility20 (20 facilities), and jalandmark10 (10 landmarks)—are bundled in a dataset by Recruit Co. under a CC‑BY‑4.0 license.

A dataset of 5,000 Japanese texts from Aozora Bunko scored for difficulty with jReadability, providing overall, kanji, lexical and detailed readability metrics for curriculum design and adaptive learning.

18,612 Japanese‑translated Python instruction–response pairs—generated with GPT‑4o‑mini, preserving original English prompts, code, and examples—offer diverse coding tasks for training, fine‑tuning, chatbots, research, and education, all released under MIT license.
JCommonsenseQA is a Japanese, multiple‑choice dataset for commonsense reasoning—an adaptation of CommonsenseQA—licensed under CC BY‑SA 4.0 and cited as doi:10.5715/jnlp.30.63.
s: Reasoning, Commonsense Reasoning, Semantic Text Processing

d: Japanese Explainable Multi‑hop Question Answering dataset featuring questions, answers, and step‑by‑step derivations linking Wikipedia articles, with updated derivation formatting and multiple version releases.

JMTEB is a Japanese text‑embedding benchmark featuring 5 tasks (clustering, classification, STS, retrieval, reranking) and 28 datasets, offering a one‑line evaluation script and inviting community contributions.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Responsible & Trustworthy NLP, Representation Learning, Text Classification, Semantic Text Processing

d: JamC‑QA is a bilingual benchmark of multiple‑choice questions spanning eight Japanese‑culture and knowledge categories, with leaderboard metrics comparing state‑of‑the‑art models.

WildGuardTestJP is a Japanese‑language evaluation dataset of 1,725 samples, faithfully translated from WildGuardTest through a multi‑stage refinement pipeline (Seed‑X‑PPO‑7B, gpt‑oss‑120b, Qwen2.5‑72B‑Instruct, gemma‑3‑27b‑it) and released on Hugging Face under the ODC‑BY license.

A dataset that quantifies the intelligence gap between voice‑ and text‑input for voice‑language models, consisting of audio synthesized from the Elyza‑tasks‑100, M‑IFEval, and JamC‑QA benchmarks into four subsets, with text under CC‑BY‑SA 4.0 and audio restricted to non‑commercial, non‑redistributab

Japanese Document Question Answering (JDocQA) is a large‑scale PDF‑based QA dataset with 5,504 documents and 11,600 annotated Japanese Q&A pairs covering yes/no, factoid, numerical, and open‑ended questions, including unanswerable instances, designed to evaluate complex comprehension of visual and t

Updated JGLUE dataset card and loading script for a Japanese NLP benchmark (created by Yahoo Japan and Waseda University) that covers text classification (MARC‑ja, JCoLA), sentence‑pair classification (JNLI), and QA (JSQuAD, JCommonsenseQA), with releases linked on GitHub and Hugging Face.

STAIR‑Captions, released in 2017, offers 820,310 Japanese captions for caption generation, multimodal retrieval, and image generation, complete with detailed annotations, metadata, and a Creative Commons BY‑4.0 license.

JSNLI is a Japanese translation of the SNLI natural‑language‑inference benchmark, provided as TSV files of morpheme‑segmented premise‑hypothesis–label triples in filtered and unfiltered training and validation splits, licensed under CC BY‑SA 4.0.

Livedoor News Corpus offers a Japanese news article dataset, split into 5 894 training, 737 validation, and 736 test instances, cleaned of HTML tags and released under a Creative Commons Attribution‑NoDerivs license.

The WRIME dataset is a Japanese collection of 42,200 posts annotated with Plutchik’s eight emotions for the writer, three readers, and their averages, structured into 40 k‑train, 1.2 k‑validation, and 2 k‑test splits for sentiment‑analysis tasks.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: WAON is a large, high‑quality Japanese image‑text pair dataset built with size, SigLIP‑score filtering and deduplication (by URLs, captions and pHash), and released on HuggingFace under Apache 2.0 for informational analysis.
Japanese translations of the neuralwork/arXiv abstract column generated with gemma‑2‑2b‑it, licensed under CC BY‑NC‑SA 4.0.
A CC‑BY‑SA 3.0 licensed dataset for Japanese named‑entity extraction derived from Wikipedia, hosted at https://github.com/stockmarkteam/ner-wikipedia-dataset/ and developed by Stockmark Inc.
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Named Entity Recognition, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: A multimodal Japanese table‑QA dataset for direct cell value extraction from annual securities report tables, providing images, OCR text with bounding boxes, questions and answers under a CC‑BY‑4.0 license.

A dataset of 64,139 Japanese names labeled with biological gender—presented in kanji, hiragana, and romaji—whose 44.9 k training, 6.41 k validation, and 12.8 k test split earned acceptance at ISDA’23.
A Japanese multi‑choice QA dataset derived from the “abc” quiz competition’s four‑option questions, released for research use under non‑commercial license.
Japanese‑translated version of the LLaVA CC3M 595K visual‑instruct dataset for research on multimodal models and chatbots.

Cauldron‑JA is a Japanese vision‑language dataset of 44 sub‑datasets translated from The Cauldron using the DeepL API, available via HuggingFace’s datasets library and licensed identically to the original set, with prompts released under CC‑BY‑4.0.
s: Multilinguality, Visual Data in NLP, Text Generation, Machine Translation, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Japanese‑Heron‑Bench supplies 21 culturally‑tagged Japanese images with 102 Q&A across Conversation, Detail, and Complex categories, benchmarked for Japanese vision‑language models.

Japanese LLaVA Pretrain is a DeepL‑transferred localized version of the original LLaVA Pretrain dataset, keeping the CC‑3M and BLIP licenses, and is aimed at researchers and hobbyists building Japanese‑language multimodal models and chatbots.
Japanese LLaVA v1.5 Instruct 620K is a DeepL‑translated subset of the 655K Visual Instruct dataset for research on large multimodal models and chatbots in Japanese, released under CC BY‑NC‑4.0.

A Japanese web collection of 56 million documents, 110 B characters and 249 million images used to train large vision‑language models—offering a momiji_generator for data population, OBELICS‑style visualization, and a sample model (Heron‑NVILA‑Lite).
s: Visual Data in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Rakuda supplies 40 Japanese questions—open‑ended for history, society, and government, and specific for geography—for benchmarking Japanese AI assistants, comparable to vicuna‑eval, and can be loaded with `datasets.load_dataset`.
Transcribed Makise Kurisu VN dialogue from a video using Whisper Large‑V2, split with pydub; uncleaned text intended for TTS model training (copyright not owned).

ELYZA‑japanese‑Llama‑2‑7b is a Japanese‑optimized extension of Meta’s Llama‑2 7 B, offering instruct and fast variants with 6.27–6.37 B parameters that can be accessed via the Hugging‑Face Transformers library.

ELYZA‑japanese‑Llama‑2‑7b is a 6.27‑B‑parameter Japanese extension of Meta’s Llama‑2‑7B, further pre‑trained for Japanese language tasks and offered in base, instruct, fast, and fast‑instruct variants, maintained by the ELYZA team under the Llama 2 Community License.
ELYZA‑japanese‑Llama‑2‑7b is a 6.27‑B‑parameter extension of Meta’s Llama‑2 model, pre‑trained on Japanese data with instruct and fast variants, and usable through Hugging Face Transformers.
Llama‑3‑ELYZA‑JP‑8B is a Japanese‑enhanced, 8‑billion‑parameter Llama 3 model by ELYZA, fine‑tuned for Japanese on Meta‑Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct.
Llama‑3‑ELYZA‑JP‑8B is a Japanese‑enhanced 8‑B Llama 3 model with GGUF (Q4_K_M) and AWQ quantization, ready to run via llama.cpp, LM Studio, or an OpenAI‑compatible API.

fastText is a lightweight, open‑source library that rapidly learns word and sentence embeddings and classifiers on a standard CPU, trains on billions of words in minutes, can be compressed for mobile use, and provides pre‑trained vectors for classification and language identification.

A Japanese‑focused DeBERTa V3 model with xsmall, base and large variants that uses no morphological analyzer at inference, respects word boundaries, employs a reduced vocabulary (BPE only in the large model), and is Hugging Face‑compatible.

A Japanese CrossEncoder reranker suite—including xsmall, small, base, large, and japanese‑bge‑reranker‑v2‑m3‑v1—paired with example usage, evaluation metrics on several benchmarks, and supporting documentation.

A Japanese Reranker v2 suite that publishes CrossEncoder and base models from tiny to large, each with benchmark scores and GPU inference times, and requires HuggingFace Transformers ≥ 4.48 (optionally flash‑attn for faster inference).

Japanese CrossEncoder Reranker models (xsmall, small, base, large, BGE‑v2 m3) with hidden sizes 384–1024, example inference via sentence_transformers and Hugging Face, scoring 0.71–0.97+ on JQaRA, JaCWIR, MIRACL, and JSQuAD.
Japanese-language CrossEncoder reranker models—from xsmall to large—trained on Japanese text, exposed through sentence_transformers, with evaluation on JQaRA, JaCWIR, MIRACL, and JSQuAD.

Japanese‑trained CrossEncoder rerankers ranging from xsmall (384) to large (1024) plus a BGE‑v2‑m3‑v1 model, with example code for fine‑tuning, inference, and benchmark scores on JQaRA, JaCWIR, MIRACL, and JSQuAD.

Japanese CrossEncoder reranker models ranging from xsmall to large (plus BGE), evaluated on JQaRA, JaCWIR, MIRACL, and JSQuAD, with ready‑to‑use integration examples for sentence_transformers and HuggingFace.

A compact, high‑speed Japanese reranker library (v2) offering tiny‑through‑base and cross‑encoder models with detailed latency and accuracy statistics, requiring Transformers ≥ 4.48 (optionally Flash‑Attention 2) and supporting ONNX/quantization for CPU/ARM deployments.

Fast, lightweight Japanese Reranker v2 models (tiny, xsmall, small, base) with benchmark scores and GPU speeds, usable via sentence_transformers CrossEncoder and transformers ≥ v4.48 (optionally accelerated with flash‑attn) and also available in ONNX/quantized forms for CPU/ARM.
High‑performance Japanese SPLADE v2 enables sparse‑vector conversion and inference through a WebUI demo, trains with YAST, offers YASEM embedding, and reports JMTEB benchmark results.

DeBERTaV2 base trained on Japanese corpora (CC‑100, mC4, OSCAR2301, Wikipedia, Wikinews) with FP‑16 fine‑tuning for NLU tasks (JSTS, JNLI, JCommonsenseQA), released under CC BY‑SA 4.0 and funded by Japanese research grants.
s: Syntactic Text Processing, Text Segmentation, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: Japanese sentiment analysis model trained on the chABSA dataset, achieving loss 0.0001, accuracy 1.0, and F1 1.0, built with Transformers 4.24.0 and PyTorch 1.12.1+cu113, optimized with Adam (learning rate 2e‑05, 10 epochs, batch size 16) and evaluated via `model(**inputs)`.
Japanese wav2vec‑2 XLSR‑53 fine‑tuned on Common Voice 6.1, CSS10, and JSUT, requiring 16 kHz audio and usable via HuggingSound or HuggingFace pipelines.
s: Representation Learning, Text Generation, Language Models, Speech & Audio in NLP, Semantic Text Processing, Multimodality

d: Japanese NER using cl‑tohoku/bert‑base‑japanese‑v2 that extracts eight entity types (corporations, political/other organizations, facilities, products, events) via `BertForTokenClassification`, trained on the Stockmark Wikipedia dataset and installable with `transformers`, `unidic_lite`, and `fugash
s: Information Extraction & Text Mining, Information Retrieval, Named Entity Recognition, Text Classification, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: Fine‑tuned from PaddleOCR‑VL, PaddleOCR‑VL‑For‑Manga achieves 70 % full‑sentence accuracy on Manga109‑s speech‑bubble crops—over triple the 27 % baseline—using a multi‑language dataset and includes training code and a developer guide.

A fine‑tuned GGUF version of Google’s Gemma 4 31B, optimized for Project Wannabe’s structured prompt format and Japanese creative writing, while retaining general dialogue and inference capabilities for use in various chat UIs.

Manga OCR is a Vision Encoder‑Decoder OCR tool that reads vertical and horizontal Japanese manga text—including furigana—across diverse fonts and low‑quality images, with the source code freely available.

Kotoba‑Whisper‑Bilingual v1.0 delivers 6.3× faster distilled Whisper models for Japanese and English ASR plus bidirectional speech‑to‑text translation, built from OpenAI’s Whisper large‑v3 via knowledge distillation with cross‑entropy and KL‑divergence loss.
s: Multilinguality, Speech Recognition, Text Generation, Machine Translation, Speech & Audio in NLP, Multimodality, Annotation and Dataset Development

d: Kotoba‑Whisper v2.0 is a Japanese ASR model distilled from OpenAI Whisper large‑v3, trained on 7.2 million ReazonSpeech clips, that runs 6.3× faster while matching the teacher’s CER/WER on in‑domain tests and includes stable‑ts/punctuation support and full training code on GitHub.
Kotoba Whisper v2.0 is converted to CTranslate2 format for use with CTranslate2 and faster‑whisper, providing installation, inference examples, Apple M2 benchmarking, and conversion instructions.
s: Speech Recognition, Speech & Audio in NLP

d: Kotoba‑Whisper‑v2.1 is a Japanese ASR model extending kotoba‑whisper‑v2.0 with integrated punctuation‑postprocessing pipelines that maintain comparable CER performance while enabling seamless, punctuation‑aware transcription.
s: Vocabulary, Dictionary, and Language Input Method

d: Kotoba‑Whisper‑v2.2 is a Japanese ASR model extending kotoba‑whisper‑v2.0 with integrated diarization and automatic punctuation via a HuggingFace‑Transformers pipeline, built in collaboration with Asahi Ushio and Kotoba Technologies.
s: Language Models, Speech & Audio in NLP, Semantic Text Processing, Multimodality

d: Japanese DeBERTa V2 base model pretrained on 171 GB of Japanese Wikipedia, CC‑100 and OSCAR data using Juman++ segmentation and SentencePiece tokenization, trained for three weeks on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs and ready for fine‑tuning.

Japanese DeBERTa‑V2 large model pre‑trained on 171 GB of Japanese text (Wikipedia, CC‑100, OSCAR) with Juman++ segmentation, trained 36 days on 8 A100 GPUs, and fine‑tunable for downstream tasks.

d: Japanese DeBERTa V2 tiny, pretrained on ~171 GB of Japanese Wikipedia, CC‑100, and OSCAR corpora, requires Juman++ word segmentation, was trained in 33 h on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, and can be fine‑tuned for downstream tasks.

d: Japanese DeBERTa V3 base pre‑trained on 540 B tokens from LLM‑jp v1.0, trained with a modified DeBERTa V3 setup, uses a unigram byte‑fallback tokenizer (no morphological analyzer), and is fine‑tuned for JGLUE NLU tasks.

Japanese character‑level GPT‑2 Large (717 M parameters) pre‑trained on ~171 GB of Japanese Wikipedia, CC‑100, and OSCAR data, trained for ~8 months on an NVIDIA A100 with AdamW optimizer and linear LR schedule, usable via Hugging Face pipelines for text generation or feature extraction.

Pretrained TinyLlama in Japanese (≈50 k steps) built on ~3 B OSCAR/mC4 tokens, usable via HuggingFace Transformers or VLLM, created by Peter Devine, Sho Higuchi, Yuuki Yamanaka, Atom Sonoda, Shunichi Taniguchi, Tomioka Wataru, and Renju Aoki.

LY Corporation’s clip‑japanese‑base is a Japanese CLIP model trained on ~1 B image‑text pairs, using an Eva02‑B transformer image encoder with a 12‑layer BERT text encoder, achieving R@1 0.30 on STAIR, 0.89 accuracy on Recruit and 0.58 accuracy on ImageNet‑1K, and supporting zero‑shot image classifi

Japanese CLIP model clip‑japanese‑base‑v2, upgraded with ~2 B image‑text pairs and distillation, pairs an Eva02‑B image encoder with a 12‑layer BERT text encoder to reach higher ImageNet‑1k accuracy (0.708) than its predecessor.

LINE DistilBERT Japanese is a 66‑million‑parameter DistilBERT model pre‑trained on 131 GB of Japanese web text using an in‑house BERT‑base teacher, evaluated on JGLUE, tokenized with MeCab Unidic and SentencePiece, and released under the Apache 2.0 license.

d: Anime Whisper is a lightweight Japanese ASR model fine‑tuned on ~5,300 h of anime‑style dialogue that delivers low hallucination, rhythm‑aligned punctuation and accurate transcription of non‑verbal sounds and NSFW content, and must be run without an initial prompt.

A Japanese BERT‑based model fine‑tuned on the JGLUE JSTS dataset for semantic similarity scoring—introduced in chapter 5 of “Large Language Model Introduction”—with Colab notebooks, transformers‑pipeline usage, and an Apache 2.0 license.

d: Japanese BERT‑Base v3 fine‑tuned on the MARC‑ja JGLUE dataset for sentiment analysis, built from cl‑tohoku/bert‑base‑japanese‑v3 and usable with Hugging Face `pipeline`, released under Apache License 2.0.

Fine‑tuned Japanese BERT‑Base for named‑entity recognition on a Wikipedia dataset, presented in Chapter 6 of the *Large Language Model Introduction* book and deployable via a Hugging Face transformers pipeline (Apache 2.0 licensed).

d: LLM‑jp offers 13 B and 1.3 B transformer language models, including multiple instruction‑tuned variants, built with Megatron‑DeepSpeed and the Hugging Face Transformers ecosystem.

Large‑language models from LLM‑jp – 13B and 1.3B Japanese‑English transformers with multiple instruction and LoRA variants, pre‑trained via Megatron‑DeepSpeed and released in Hugging Face format (torch ≥ 2.0, transformers ≥ 4.34).

Japanese 150‑M‑parameter instructional LLM from Japan’s National Institute of Informatics, available as a Hugging Face‑compatible Transformer model requiring torch ≥ 2.3.0 and transformers ≥ 4.40.1.

LLM‑jp‑3.1‑13b‑instruct4 is a 13‑B, instruction‑pre‑trained Japanese language model developed by NII’s R&D Center, released as a Hugging‑Face Transformers checkpoint with a UNIGRAM‑byte‑fallback tokenizer.

A 32‑billion‑parameter Japanese transformer LLM (llm‑jp‑4‑32b‑a3b‑thinking) from the National Institute of Informatics, pre‑trained and aligned via supervised fine‑tuning and direct preference optimization—no reinforcement learning—using a unigram byte‑fallback tokenizer.

A repository that hosts the 8.6 B‑parameter llm‑jp‑4‑8b‑base transformer from the National Institute of Informatics’ LLM R&D Center, trained with pre‑training and mid‑training followed by supervised fine‑tuning and direct preference optimization (no reinforcement learning), and provides a PyTorch‑tr

llm‑jp‑4‑8b‑instruct is a 4.1 B‑parameter Japanese LLM from NII’s LLM‑jp‑4 series, pretrained on large corpora and then fine‑tuned solely with supervised instruction data (no DPO/REINFORCE) and accompanied by cookbook‑style usage guides and a byte‑fallback unigram tokenizer.

Provides the 8 B‑parameter LLM‑jp‑4‑8b‑thinking Japanese language model from NII, trained with pre‑/mid‑training and aligned via SFT/DPO, ready to use with torch‑transformers and detailed cookbook instructions.

LLM‑jp‑Moshi‑v1 is an experimental Japanese full‑duplex voice‑chat model built on the 7‑B‑parameter Moshi architecture, fine‑tuned with J‑CHAT and Zoom dialogue data, and released with a web‑UI demo for Linux GPU systems under the Apache 2.0 license.

A LLaMA 3 Youko qlora fine‑tune built on a new VNTL dataset, optimized for accurate, literal translations of Japanese visual novels to English without chat mode, using the default LLaMA 3 prompt and recommending neutral sampling (temperature 0, no repetition penalty).

Manga OCR is a Vision Encoder‑Decoder system that delivers high‑quality OCR of Japanese manga—including vertical and horizontal text with furigana overlays—across diverse fonts and low‑quality images, and can also be used for general printed Japanese OCR.

ja_ginza_electra is a spaCy v3 Python package offering a Japanese ELECTRA model fine‑tuned on mC4 and UD_Japanese_BCCWJ r2.8 (based on megagonlabs/transformers‑ud‑japanese‑electra‑base‑discrimininator) with custom bunsetu‑phrase detection, distributed under the MIT license.
GGUF‑formatted NVIDIA‑Nemotron‑Nano‑9B‑v2‑Japanese, rebuilt from the imatrix dataset, and ready to run with llama.cpp on CUDA.
GGUF‑formatted version of Qwen3‑Swallow‑8B‑RL‑v0.2 built with imatrix Japanese LLM data, ready for CUDA‑enabled llama.cpp use.
A gguf‑converted Qwen3‑Swallow‑8B‑SFT‑v0.2 model for Japanese LLM, built using imatrix data, with llama.cpp instructions for inference.
A GGUF‑compiled version of LLM‑JP’s 4.8‑b “thinking” model, built with llama.cpp using the imatrix dataset and a custom chat template.

Experimental Japanese model created by extracting differences between lightblue/suzume‑llama‑3‑8B‑japanese and Meta‑Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct using a chat‑vector approach, upsampled and applied to Meta‑Llama‑3‑70B‑Instruct, showing little change and planning future scaling.

A gguf-format release of moonshotai’s Moonlight‑16B‑A3B‑Instruct, trained on TFMC’s imatrix Japanese dataset, ready for use with llama.cpp (CUDA‑enabled) and shown by running a recipe‑request prompt.

A GGUF‑format release of pfnet’s plamo‑2‑translate built from imatrix data based on the TFMC/imatrix‑dataset‑for‑japanese‑LLM, with instructions to compile and run it via llama.cpp on CUDA‑enabled hardware.
Repository hosts the gguf-format conversion of tokyotech‑llm’s Llama‑3.1‑Swallow‑8B‑Instruct‑v0.3 model, built with the imatrix dataset and ready to run with llama.cpp.

Fine‑tuned Qwen3‑ASR‑1.7B for Japanese ASR, optimized to accurately transcribe proper nouns, organization and product names, kanji‑heavy and mixed Japanese/English technical terms.

Japanese RoBERTa‑base pretrained on Japanese Wikipedia and Japanese CC‑100, using Juman++ word segmentation and SentencePiece tokenization, trained in a week on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with Adam (lr = 1e‑4, native AMP), and fine‑tunable with results reported on JGLUE.

A 9‑billion‑parameter Japanese‑optimized LLM, NVIDIA Nemotron‑Nano‑9B‑v2‑Japanese, trained on data up to September 2024 with a hybrid Mamba‑2/MLP/4‑layer‑attention architecture, fine‑tuned on the Nemotron‑Personas‑Japan tool‑calling dataset, that optionally generates a controllable reasoning trace b

d: sbert-jsnli‑luke‑japanese‑base‑lite is a 768‑dimensional sentence‑transformer built on studio‑ousia/luke‑japanese‑base‑lite, trained one epoch on shunk031/jsnli, and includes examples for clustering, semantic search, and both Sentence‑Transformers and HuggingFace usage.
s: Representation Learning, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

d: A Japanese BERT BASE fine‑tuned on the WRIME dataset predicts 0‑4 intensity scores for eight emotions (joy, sadness, anticipation, surprise, anger, fear, disgust, trust) for writers and readers, with code available, trained in 3 hrs on a K80, achieving about 0.6 MSE for writers and 0.2 MSE for reade
s: Language Models, Emotion Analysis, Semantic Text Processing, Sentiment Analysis

d: PLaMo Translation Model is a large‑scale language model created by Preferred Networks for translation tasks, available in base, post‑trained, and evaluation variants, released under the PLaMo community license and not instruction‑tuned for chat or other downstream uses.

PLaMo‑Embedding‑1B is a Japanese text‑embedding model from Preferred Networks that converts Japanese text into vectors for information retrieval, classification, and clustering, shows strong performance on the JMTEB benchmark, and is freely available under an Apache v2.0 license.

GLuCoSE is a Japanese sentence‑embedding model built on LUKE that outputs 768‑dim mean‑pooled vectors (up to 512 tokens) trained on web and NLI/search data, achieving 0.864 Spearman and 0.818 Pearson on similarity benchmarks.

GLuCoSE v2 is a CPU‑friendly Japanese text‑embedding model, fine‑tuned by distillation and multi‑stage contrastive learning, that delivers superior semantic‑similarity and retrieval performance—outperforming comparable‑size models on MIRACL and related benchmarks.
s: Representation Learning, Information Retrieval, Semantic Text Processing

d: Fine‑tuned Japanese HuBERT‑base for CTC phoneme recognition (v3), adding MeCab N‑best with pyopenjtalk‑plus post‑processing, a weighted loss combining CTC and MeCab costs, updated exclusion rules, and a new “ty” phoneme.

d: Fine‑tuned Japanese Hubert‑Base for CTC phoneme recognition (v4) with updated sentence‑filtering rules, pronunciation adjustments, and a GPU switch to A6000, stopped training at 110k steps.

A 12‑layer, 768‑hidden Japanese GPT‑NeoX model trained on CC‑100, C4, and Wikipedia, compatible with Huggingface, with an optional toy prefix‑tuning weight that forces each sentence to end with a smiling‑face emoji.

Rinna’s 24‑layer, 1024‑hidden Japanese GPT‑2‑medium model, trained on CC‑100 and Wikipedia with SentencePiece tokenization, is available in the rinna/japanese‑pretrained‑models repo (MIT‑licensed, released April 7 2021, updated 25 Aug 2021).

meikiocr supplies a D‑FINE‑based, open‑weight text‑detection model for video‑games (v0.1 with MobileNet‑v4 backbones, two resolution variants and a 64‑box limit) and experimental low‑latency tiny and small variants trained on Japanese video‑games and manga.

Meikiocr’s `meiki.text.recognition.v0`—a D‑FINE-based MobileNetV4 model fine‑tuned on Japanese video‑game text—delivers state‑of‑the‑art accuracy and latency for horizontal text by detecting up to 48 characters from 960×32 inputs, outputting each character with its bounding box and confidence score.

SakuraLLM delivers non‑commercial, RLHF‑fine‑tuned Japanese‑to‑Chinese translation models for light novel and galgame domains, built on open‑source LLMs with domain‑specific data, offering multiple sizes, offline deployment, and clear licence and usage guidelines.
DiaFill is a fine‑tuned Japanese two‑speaker dialogue script generator that produces natural, filler‑rich spoken‑style conversations from seed prompts, trained on proprietary GENIAC data.

A 132‑million‑parameter Japanese ModernBERT model that blends local‑global and RoPE attention, trained on 4.39 T tokens (Japanese/English) with a 102‑k‑size vocab, 8,192‑token max length, and optimized for Flash Attention 2.

ModernBERT‑Ja‑30M is a Japanese‑language BERT variant that blends local and global attention with RoPE, trained on 4.39 TB of Japanese/English text, supports 8,192‑token sequences, comes in sizes from 30 M to 130 M parameters, and works best with Flash Attention 2.

ModernBERT‑Ja‑310M is a Japanese‑language BERT variant that blends local‑global attention with RoPE, trained on 4.09 T tokens of Japanese/English text, supports a 102 400‑word vocabulary, 8 192‑token sequences, and is optimized for Flash Attention 2.

Offers an autoregressive Japanese language model (sarashina2.2‑3B‑instruct‑v0.1) from SB Intuitions, benchmarked against other models, with example usage scripts and a note that safety training is limited.

d: Sarashina2.2‑OCR is a 3‑B parameter, end‑to‑end OCR model that, after human‑preference optimization, parses Japanese and English documents into Markdown while converting tables to HTML, math to LaTeX, and graphics into bounding‑box annotations, and achieves this by integrating a SigLIP2‑based vision

sarashina2.2-tts is an SB Intuitions LLM‑based, Japanese‑centric TTS system delivering high‑accuracy Japanese and English synthesis with natural expressive voice, zero‑shot cloning, cross‑lingual consistency, and seamless code‑switching.
Sarashina2.2‑Vision‑3B is a 3‑B parameter Japanese large vision‑language model built on Sarashina2.2‑3B‑Instruct and a SigLIP image encoder, achieving strong performance on Japanese VQA benchmarks.
Japanese GPT‑2 lyric generation model trained on 143,587 uta‑net songs, offered via a web demo and Torch/Transformers usage example.

Japanese Sentence‑LUKE model trained on the same dataset as Sentence‑BERT, outperforming or matching it, built on studio‑ousia/luke‑japanese‑base‑lite and used via Hugging Face Transformers’ MLukeTokenizer and LukeModel.

FuguMT is a Marian‑NMT based English‑to‑Japanese translation model built with Hugging Face Transformers and SentencePiece, achieving a BLEU score of 32.7 on Tatoeba.
FuguMT is a Japanese‑to‑English Marian‑NMT translation model built with transformers and SentencePiece, scoring 39.1 BLEU on Tatoeba.

s: Representation Learning, Syntactic Text Processing, Text Segmentation, Language Models, Semantic Text Processing

Japanese‑enhanced Gemma‑2 “Swallow” language models built with continual pre‑training and instruction‑tuned fine‑tuning, available in 2 b, 9 b, and 27 b variants on Hugging Face.

Llama 3.1 Swallow is a Japanese‑enhanced series of 8B/70B Llama 3.1 models, trained via continual pre‑training and Japanese‑specific instruction fine‑tuning, with the latest 8B‑Instruct‑v0.3 setting state‑of‑the‑art results on Japanese MT‑Bench.

Llama 3.1 Swallow is a set of 8‑B and 70‑B models that continue pre‑training Meta’s Llama 3.1 to boost Japanese language performance, then instruction‑fine‑tune on synthetic Japanese data—providing multiple released variants with improved conversational behavior comparable to gemma‑3‑27b‑it.
